Jeremiah is commanded to become a living sign of coming devastation. He cannot marry, cannot mourn, cannot celebrate—he must remove himself from the ordinary rhythms of human life that bring comfort and continuity. This is not cruelty for cruelty's sake. God is making Jeremiah's very existence a message: what you take for granted will be taken from you. The comfort of family, the solidarity of grief shared with others, the joy of wedding feasts—all will vanish from Jerusalem. The text is unflinching about what this looks like: corpses left exposed like dung, death by disease and famine, the silence of a people too broken to properly mourn their dead. God removes His peace, His steadfast love, His mercy from the land. This is what it means to be abandoned by God, and Jeremiah must embody this abandonment before a people who refuse to see it coming.

When they ask why—and they will ask—the answer is clear and damning. Their fathers turned away and followed other gods, and they themselves have done worse. They have not merely inherited idolatry; they have actively chosen stubbornness over obedience, their own evil hearts over God's instruction. There is no excuse, no misunderstanding, no tragedy that befell them without warning. They have knowingly rejected their God while clinging to worthless idols. The judgment is therefore just, though terrible: exile to a land they do not know, to serve gods they do not choose, with no favor from the One who brought them out of Egypt. This is the consequence of deliberate rebellion.

Yet even here, in the middle of judgment, God speaks of restoration. The days will come when the deliverance from Egypt will no longer be the defining act of God's salvation—when instead, the return from exile itself will become the new declaration of His faithfulness. But before that restoration, judgment must run its course. God will hunt them down as fishermen and hunters pursue their prey on every mountain and in every crevice. They cannot hide; His eyes see all their ways, and their guilt is not concealed from Him. He will repay them double for defiling His land with the carcasses of their idols. The weight of this is staggering: judgment commensurate with the depth of their sin.

In this darkness, the reflection ends not with the prophet's despair but with the nation's future confession. The nations themselves will come to see the futility of idolatry—that men cannot make gods, that these are lies and worthless things. And when God finally acts to make known His power and might, they will know that He alone is the LORD. Judgment serves this purpose: not merely punishment, but the revelation of God's true character to a world that has forgotten Him. The exile will accomplish what words could not. The removal of everything they thought they could depend on will teach them, at last, to depend on Him.
